metadata.summary: REST API patterns for DRF and Ninja: ViewSet vs APIView, serializer design, JWT auth, throttling, pagination. Core: thin views, validate with serializers, delegate to services.

content.title: Django API Development
content.summary: Patterns for building REST APIs with Django REST Framework (DRF 3.15+) or Django Ninja 1.x. Covers ViewSet vs APIView selection, serializer/schema design, JWT auth, throttling, pagination, filtering, and OpenAPI documentation. Core rule: keep views thin — validate input with serializers, delegate business logic to a service layer, return typed responses.

why.p: Fat views that mix validation, business logic, and side effects are the single biggest source of Django API bugs and test failures. Separating concerns (serializer → service → view) makes each layer independently testable and reduces the surface area for security mistakes like `fields = "__all__"` leaking new columns.

list.item: Building new REST endpoints on an existing Django 5.x project.
Choosing between DRF and Ninja at project bootstrap.
Adding CRUD ViewSets or custom APIView actions to an existing DRF codebase.
Implementing or hardening JWT/OAuth2/API-key auth, throttling, or cursor pagination.
Generating OpenAPI specs for client SDKs via `drf-spectacular` or Ninja's built-in spec.
Greenfield project with no Django code and no admin requirement — FastAPI is usually a better fit.
Pure GraphQL APIs — use `graphene-django` / `strawberry-django`; this covers REST only.
Internal RPC services — gRPC + protobuf; REST overhead is not justified.

section.name: framework-selection
section.p: Decision rules for choosing DRF vs Django Ninja, and ViewSet vs APIView within DRF.

rule.statement: Choose DRF when the project requires a large ecosystem, complex permission hierarchies, or team familiarity with Django class-based views.
rule.rationale: DRF has 14 years of battle-testing, 1000+ third-party packages, built-in browsable API, and plugin stubs (django-stubs, djangorestframework-stubs) for mypy. Django Ninja has a smaller ecosystem (since 2021).

rule.statement: Choose Django Ninja for new greenfield projects that require native async, Pydantic v2 validation, or FastAPI-like ergonomics.
rule.rationale: Ninja's async path is first-class; adding async to DRF via adrf is bolted-on and produces sync-looking code that can silently not be async. Ninja is ~2-3x faster in high-concurrency benchmarks due to async I/O, not request overhead.

rule.statement: Never mix DRF and Ninja in the same Django app — URL conflicts and authentication context diverge.
rule.rationale: Lock the choice at project bootstrap. Mixing multiplies the failure surface and makes OpenAPI spec generation inconsistent.

rule.statement: Use ModelViewSet for standard CRUD operations on a single model with DefaultRouter URLs.
rule.rationale: ViewSets reduce boilerplate: list/create/retrieve/update/partial_update/destroy are generated from a queryset and serializer class. DefaultRouter handles URL naming automatically.

rule.statement: Use APIView for action verbs (activate, archive, retry) or endpoints that aggregate multiple models.
rule.rationale: APIView gives full control over HTTP method dispatch without forcing router URL patterns. Use when the endpoint doesn't map cleanly to a single model resource.

example.title: ViewSet for standard CRUD: list, create, retrieve, update, destroy on Product
APIView for a custom action verb: publish

section.name: serializers-schemas
section.p: Rules for DRF serializer separation and Ninja schema design that prevent security leaks and testing friction.

rule.statement: Use separate input serializers (validation) and output serializers (representation) — never one serializer for both.
rule.rationale: A single serializer that handles both input and output forces compromises: write-only fields mix with read-only fields, and changing one breaks the other. Separate serializers also make permission-based field differences explicit.

rule.statement: Never use Meta.fields = "__all__" on any user-facing serializer.
rule.rationale: New columns added to the model are automatically exposed to API consumers. This has caused accidental password hash, token, and internal ID leaks in production systems.

rule.statement: List fields explicitly in Meta.fields and declare read_only_fields for computed and auto-set fields.
rule.rationale: An allowlist prevents new columns from leaking. read_only_fields ensures id, uid, created_at, updated_at cannot be set via PATCH/PUT.

example.title: Single serializer with __all__ — exposes every field including internal ones
Separate input and output serializers with explicit field lists

rule.statement: Use ModelSchema for output; use Schema (Pydantic) for input with explicit validators.
rule.rationale: ModelSchema auto-generates fields from the Django model for output, which is safe for read paths. Input schemas need explicit Pydantic validators to enforce business rules.

example.title: Ninja: separate Create schema (input) and ModelSchema (output)

section.name: auth-permissions-throttle
section.p: Configuration rules for JWT auth, object-level permissions, scoped throttling, and cursor pagination.

rule.statement: Configure simplejwt with 15-minute access tokens, 7-day refresh tokens, token rotation, and blacklisting enabled.
rule.rationale: Short access token lifetimes limit the blast radius of a leaked token. Rotation + blacklisting prevents refresh token replay after logout.

rule.statement: Store JWT tokens in HttpOnly cookies, not localStorage — or use dj-rest-auth for cookie middleware wiring.
rule.rationale: localStorage is accessible to JavaScript; XSS attacks can steal tokens. HttpOnly cookies are not accessible from JS and are sent automatically with same-origin requests.

rule.statement: Set IsAuthenticated as the global default; never set AllowAny as the default permission class.
rule.rationale: AllowAny as default means any new endpoint is publicly accessible until explicitly protected. A locked-down default forces explicit opt-in to public access.

rule.statement: Object-level permissions go in has_object_permission; permission_classes handles only route-level (method + auth) checks.
rule.rationale: has_permission runs before the object is fetched — cheap. has_object_permission runs after get_object() — allows ownership/org membership checks per resource.

example.title: Object-level permission: user must belong to object's organization

rule.statement: Throttle by scope, not only anon/user. Add a burst scope for write endpoints and a login scope for auth endpoints.
rule.rationale: A single anon/user rate limit cannot differentiate between a safe read flood and a brute-force login attempt. Scoped throttling applies tighter limits where it matters.

rule.statement: Use cursor pagination only on indexed ordering fields (e.g., (-created_at, -id)); always paginate list endpoints.
rule.rationale: Cursor pagination on an unindexed field causes full table scans. The -id tie-breaker is required for stable ordering when multiple rows share the same created_at timestamp.

section.name: antipatterns
section.p: Concrete failure modes that agents and developers consistently introduce into Django REST APIs.

antipattern.description: Fat view: business logic (DB queries, balance checks, email sending) written directly in the view's post() method.
antipattern.reason: Fat views cannot be unit-tested without an HTTP request. Business logic in views is also unreachable from management commands, Celery tasks, or other entry points.

antipattern.description: Business logic placed in serializer validate_* methods instead of a service function.
antipattern.reason: Serializers should validate shape and type only. Logic in validate_* is hard to test in isolation and creates hidden coupling between validation and business rules.

antipattern.description: Using Meta.fields = "__all__" on a user-facing serializer.
antipattern.reason: New model fields (passwords, tokens, internal IDs) are automatically exposed when the model changes. An explicit allowlist is the only safe approach.

antipattern.description: Using Serializer(data=request.data, partial=True) for PATCH without a separate UpdateSerializer.
antipattern.reason: Reusing the create serializer with partial=True allows required fields to be cleared — violating invariants. A separate Update serializer makes all fields optional explicitly.
The browsable API changes content negotiation behavior (returns HTML when Accept: */* is sent), turns CSRF into a surprise vector, and leaks API structure to unauthenticated users.

antipattern.description: Writing async def methods in plain DRF views without adrf — the method runs synchronously.
antipattern.reason: DRF's dispatch pipeline is synchronous. An async def view handler is called in a sync context; it appears to work but blocks the event loop in ASGI deployments.

antipattern.description: Adding URLPathVersioning to REST_FRAMEWORK settings without adding <version> to the URL pattern.
antipattern.reason: The versioning class is applied but request.version is None — no error, silent failure. Tests must assert request.version is populated, not just that the endpoint responds.

template.purpose: Complete REST_FRAMEWORK + SIMPLE_JWT + SPECTACULAR_SETTINGS config block.
Thin APIView: validate → service → return pattern.
Ninja router with ModelSchema, AuthBearer, CRUD endpoints.
